name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ أَتَذَرُ مُوسَىٰ وَقَوْمَهُۥ لِيُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَيَذَرَكَ وَءَالِهَتَكَ ۚ قَالَ سَنُقَتِّلُ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ وَنَسْتَحْىِۦ نِسَآءَهُمْ وَإِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قَـٰهِرُونَ (١٢٧)
```

```english
The elite of Pharaoh’s people said, ‘Will you leave Moses and his people to cause corruption in the land, and to abandon you and your gods?’ He said, ‘We will kill their sons and spare their women, and indeed we are dominant over them.’ (127)
```

```arabic
قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِ ٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ يُورِثُهَا مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٢٨)
```

```english
Moses said to his people, ‘Turn to Allah for help and be patient. The earth indeed belongs to Allah, and He gives its inheritance to whomever He wishes of His servants, and the outcome will be in favour of the Godwary.’ (128)
```

```arabic
قَالُوٓا۟ أُوذِينَا مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَأْتِيَنَا وَمِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جِئْتَنَا ۚ قَالَ عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يُهْلِكَ عَدُوَّكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٢٩)
```

```english
They said, ‘We were tormented before you came to us and [also] after you came to us.’ He said, ‘Maybe your Lord will destroy your enemy and make you successors in the land, and then He will see how you act.’ (129)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ بِٱلسِّنِينَ وَنَقْصٍ مِّنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (١٣٠)
```

```english
Certainly We afflicted Pharaoh’s clan with droughts and loss of produce, so that they may take admonition. (130)
```

```arabic
فَإِذَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْحَسَنَةُ قَالُوا۟ لَنَا هَـٰذِهِۦ ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَطَّيَّرُوا۟ بِمُوسَىٰ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥٓ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّمَا طَـٰٓئِرُهُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٣١)
```

```english
But whenever any good came to them, they would say, ‘This is our due.’ And if any ill visited them, they took it for ill omens attending Moses and those who were with him. (Look! Indeed the cause of their ill omens is from Allah, but most of them do not know.) (131)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ مَهْمَا تَأْتِنَا بِهِۦ مِنْ ءَايَةٍ لِّتَسْحَرَنَا بِهَا فَمَا نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٣٢)
```

```english
And they said, ‘Whatever sign you may bring us to bewitch us, we are not going to believe you.’ (132)
```

```arabic
فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلطُّوفَانَ وَٱلْجَرَادَ وَٱلْقُمَّلَ وَٱلضَّفَادِعَ وَٱلدَّمَ ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّفَصَّلَـٰتٍ فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ (١٣٣)
```

```english
So We sent against them a flood and locusts, lice, frogs and blood, as distinct signs. But they acted arrogantly, and they were a guilty lot. (133)
```

```arabic
وَلَمَّا وَقَعَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلرِّجْزُ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَى ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ بِمَا عَهِدَ عِندَكَ ۖ لَئِن كَشَفْتَ عَنَّا ٱلرِّجْزَ لَنُؤْمِنَنَّ لَكَ وَلَنُرْسِلَنَّ مَعَكَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (١٣٤)
```

```english
Whenever a plague fell upon them, they would say, ‘O Moses, invoke your Lord for us by the covenant He has made with you. If you remove the plague from us, we will certainly believe in you and let the Children of Israel go along with you.’ (134)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمُ ٱلرِّجْزَ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ هُم بَـٰلِغُوهُ إِذَا هُمْ يَنكُثُونَ (١٣٥)
```

```english
But when We had removed the plague from them until a term that they should have completed, behold, they broke their promise. (135)
```

```arabic
فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَكَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا غَـٰفِلِينَ (١٣٦)
```

```english
So We took vengeance on them and drowned them in the sea, for they denied Our signs and were oblivious to them. (136)
```

```arabic
وَأَوْرَثْنَا ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشَـٰرِقَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَغَـٰرِبَهَا ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا ۖ وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ ۖ وَدَمَّرْنَا مَا كَانَ يَصْنَعُ فِرْعَوْنُ وَقَوْمُهُۥ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْرِشُونَ (١٣٧)
```

```english
We made the people who were abased the heirs to the east and west of the land which We had blessed, and your Lord’s best word [of promise] was fulfilled for the Children of Israel because of their patience, and We destroyed what Pharaoh and his people had built and what they used to erect. (137)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses encompass a summary of what transpired between Moses (peace be upon him) and Pharaoh and his people during the days of his stay among them after undertaking the mission—calling them to Allah and to the release of the Children of Israel. He brought them sign after sign until Allah the Exalted delivered him and his people, drowned Pharaoh and his hosts, and gave the Children of Israel inheritance of the blessed land, its easts and its wests.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَقالَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْ قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ أَتَذَرُ مُوسى وَقَوْمَهُ **"The elite of Pharaoh’s people said, ‘Will you leave Moses and his people...’"** until the end of the verse. This was an incitement (إغراء) and provocation (تحريض) on their part for Pharaoh to kill Moses and his people. Therefore, Pharaoh responded to their words by indicating that their killing does not concern us, for we are dominant over them in any case; rather, we shall reinstate our previous torment upon them, killing their sons and sparing their women. Had their request been for absolute torment other than killing, his saying: وَإِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قاهِرُونَ **"and indeed we are dominant over them"** would not have been so fittingly placed.

Their saying: وَيَذَرَكَ وَآلِهَتَكَ **"and [to] abandon you and your gods"** is an emphasis of their provocation to kill them. The meaning is that Moses abandons you and your gods, refusing to worship you, while he and his people spread corruption in the land. This contains an indication that just as Pharaoh claimed divinity (الألوهية) and enslaved people to himself, he also worshipped other gods. This is indeed the case, and history confirms similar instances among ancient nations. It has been reported that the heads of households and masters of people in Rome and other kingdoms were worshipped by their subordinates from their households and clans, while they themselves worshipped their early ancestors and other idols, just as the weak worshipped them. Furthermore, among the lords (الأرباب) worshipped in polytheism (الوثنية), there are those who are lords to other lords, or a lord to another lord, such as the lordship of the father and mother over the son, and so forth.

However, his saying to his people, as recounted by Allah, the Glorified: أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلى **"I am your exalted lord"** (79:24), and his saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي **"I do not know of any god for you other than myself"** (28:38), apparently suggests that he did not take a lord for himself and commanded his people not to worship anyone but him. For this reason, some have said that he was a materialist (دهرياً) who did not acknowledge a Creator (صانع) and commanded his people to abandon the worship of gods entirely, restricting worship to himself. Consequently, some—as it is said—read "and your worship" (وإلاهتك) with a *kasrah* on the *hamzah* and a *fathah* on the *lam*, similar to the word "worship" (العبادة) in weight and meaning.

But the more plausible view is that by his saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي **"I do not know of any god for you other than myself,"** he intended to negate any god specific to his Coptic people who would possess them and manage their affairs other than himself. This is consistent with the established beliefs of polytheists (الوثنيين) that every class of creation—such as the heavens, the earth, the land, and the sea—and every specific people, or every type of event and matter—such as peace, war, love, and beauty—has a distinct lord (رب). They only worshipped among them those whose worship concerned them, such as the inhabitants of seacoasts worshipping the lord of the sea and the storm.

Thus, the meaning of his speech is: "I am your lord, O assembly of Copts, not that which Moses has taken, claiming He is your lord who sent him to you." This is supported by the surrounding context of his saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي **"I do not know of any god for you other than myself,"** for the Exalted says: **"Pharaoh said, ‘O elite! I do not know of any god for you other than myself. So kindle for me, O Haman, [a fire] upon the clay, and build for me a tower so that I may have a look at the God of Moses, though I indeed consider him to be a liar’"** (28:38). The apparent meaning is that he was in doubt regarding Him being a God for Moses, and that the meaning of his saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي **"I do not know of any god for you other than myself"** is a negation of knowledge of the existence of another god, not the knowledge of the non-existence of another god. In short, his speech does not negate a god other than himself.

As for the possibility of Pharaoh being a materialist (دهرياً) who did not believe in the existence of the Creator (الصانع), it appears that this is found in the words of al-Razi, who said in *al-Tafsir al-Kabir* verbatim:

"What occurs to my mind is that if we say Pharaoh was not of sound mind, it would not be permissible in the wisdom of Allah the Exalted to send a messenger to him. If he was sane, it would not be possible for him to believe himself to be the creator of the heavens and the earth, nor would it be possible for a vast assembly of rational people to believe that of him, for its falsity is known by rational necessity (ضرورة العقل).

Rather, it is more likely to say: he was a materialist (دهرياً) who denied the existence of the Creator (الصانع), and he used to say: the manager of this lower world is the celestial bodies (الكواكب). As for the one who benefits the creation in this world and that particular group, and their nurturer, it was himself. Thus, his saying: أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلى **'I am your exalted lord'** means: your nurturer, your benefactor, and your provider.

And his saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي **'I do not know of any god for you other than myself'** means: I do not know of anyone for you whom you must worship except me.

If that was his doctrine, it is not far-fetched to say that he had taken idols in the forms of the celestial bodies, worshipping them and seeking proximity to them according to the religion of the star-worshippers. On this estimation, there is no impossibility in taking the saying of the Exalted: وَيَذَرَكَ وَآلِهَتَكَ **'and [to] abandon you and your gods'** according to its literal meaning. This is what I have on this matter." [End of quote].

He has erred in this, for the meaning of divinity (الألوهية) and lordship (الربوبية) among the polytheists and star-worshippers is not the creation of the heavens and the earth, but rather the management (تدبير) of some affairs of the world, as he himself suggested later. Nor is there among the materialists (الدهريين) anyone who worships the stars, nor among the Sabians and star-worshippers anyone who denies the existence of the Creator (الصانع).

Rather, the truth is that Pharaoh—as previously mentioned—saw himself as a lord (رب) over Egypt and its people, and he only denied their being subjects (مربوبي) to another god according to their own principles, not that they or the rest of the world were not created by Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ سَنُقَتِّلُ أَبْناءَهُمْ وَنَسْتَحْيِي نِساءَهُمْ وَإِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قاهِرُونَ **"He said, ‘We will kill their sons and spare their women, and indeed we are dominant over them’"** is a promise from him to the elite of his people to reinstate his previous torment upon the Children of Israel—killing their sons and sparing their women to keep them for service. He followed this with his saying: وَإِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قاهِرُونَ **"and indeed we are dominant over them"** to reassure their hearts and calm the agitation and recklessness in their souls.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالَ مُوسى لِقَوْمِهِ اسْتَعِينُوا بِاللهِ وَاصْبِرُوا **"Moses said to his people, ‘Seek help from Allah and be patient...’"** until the end of the verse. This is an exhortation from Moses (peace be upon him) to the Children of Israel and an urging for them to seek help from Allah toward their objective—which was deliverance from the captivity and enslavement of the House of Pharaoh. Then he urged them to be patient (الصبر) regarding the hardships with which Pharaoh threatened them, consisting of various forms of torment. Patience is the harbinger of good and the vanguard of every relief. He then justified this by saying: إِنَّ الْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ يُورِثُها مَنْ يَشاءُ **"Indeed the earth belongs to Allah; He gives it as an inheritance to whomever He wishes."**

The gist of it is that Pharaoh does not own the earth such that he may grant it to whom he wishes or prevent whom he wishes from enjoying it; rather, it belongs to Allah, who gives it as an inheritance to whomever He wishes. The divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — *the established way of Allah's action in creation* — has proceeded to grant the good end (حسن العاقبة) specifically to those of His servants who possess godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against His displeasure*. Thus, if you seek help from Allah and remain patient for the sake of Allah regarding the hardships that threaten you—which is godwariness—He will give you inheritance of the land that you see in the hands of the House of Pharaoh.

For this reason, He followed His saying: إِنَّ الْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ **"Indeed the earth belongs to Allah"** with His saying: وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ **"and the outcome will be in favor of the God-conscious."** The "outcome" (العاقبة) is that which follows a thing, just as the "beginning" (البادئة) is that which starts a thing. The fact that the outcome belongs absolutely to the God-conscious is because the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) dictates this. This is because the Exalted has ordered the cosmos in a system that leads every species to the goal of its existence and the felicity (سعادة) for which it was created. If it proceeds upon its path (صراط) upon which it was fashioned, and does not deviate from the line of its trajectory drawn for it, it will inevitably reach the goal of its felicity. Man, who is one of these species, is also in this state; if he proceeds upon his path (صراط) drawn for him by the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and guards against deviating from it or transgressing from it to a path other than the way of Allah through disbelief in His signs and corruption in His land, Allah will guide him to his good outcome, grant him a pleasant life, and direct him to every good he seeks.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالُوا أُوذِينا مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَأْتِيَنا وَمِنْ بَعْدِ ما جِئْتَنا **"They said, ‘We were oppressed before you came to us and after you have come to us.’"** The words "coming" (الإتيان) and "arriving" (المجيء) in the verse carry the same meaning; the variation in expression is for literary variety (تفنن). What has been said—that the meaning is "before you came to us with the signs and after you brought them to us"—has no evidence for the implied omission (تقدير) it contains. Furthermore, their purpose was to show that the coming of Moses—while they had been promised that Allah would deliver them by his hand from the calamity of captivity and the abyss of humiliation—had not produced its effect, for the harm they were bearing and being afflicted with remained as it was. Their purpose had no connection to him bringing them signs at all. This speech is a complaint from them which they were venting to Moses (peace be upon him).

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالَ عَسى رَبُّكُمْ أَنْ يُهْلِكَ عَدُوَّكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ **"He said, ‘Maybe your Lord will destroy your enemy and make you successors in the land, then He will see how you act.’"** This is a response from Moses to their saying: أُوذِينا **"We were oppressed..."** etc., with which he consoles them and comforts them with hope. It is, in reality, a repetition of his previous saying: اسْتَعِينُوا بِاللهِ وَاصْبِرُوا إِنَّ الْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ **"Seek help from Allah and be patient... Indeed the earth belongs to Allah."** It is as if he is saying: "What I commanded you—to be conscious of Allah in the pursuit of your objective—is a living and firm word. If you act upon it, it is hoped that Allah will destroy your enemy and make you successors in the land by giving it to you as an inheritance. He does not choose you for succession (الاستخلاف) as a haphazard selection, nor does He honor you with an absolute honoring without condition or restriction; rather, it is to test you with this sovereignty and try you with this empowerment and succession, to see how you act." The Exalted said: **"These are the days that We alternate among the people, and so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith, and that He may take witnesses from among you"** (3:140).

This is among the things with which the Quran corrects the belief of the Jews regarding their being honored by Allah with an honor that accepts no dismissal and bears no condition or restriction. The Torah considers the people of Israel to be the people of Allah for whom is the Holy Land, as if they owned it from Allah, the Glorified, with an ownership that accepts neither transfer nor revocation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ بِالسِّنِينَ وَنَقْصٍ مِنَ الثَّمَراتِ **"Certainly We afflicted Pharaoh’s clan with droughts and loss of fruits."** The word "years" (السنون) is the plural of "year" (سنة), meaning famine and drought. Its origin was "the year of famine," then it was said "the year" as an allusion to it, until the usage became so frequent that "the year" (السنة) became designated for the meaning of famine and drought.

Allah, the Glorified, mentions in the verse—and swears—that He afflicted the House of Pharaoh, who were his specific people from the Copts, with multiple famines and loss of fruits, so that they might take admonition.

These are two types of signs (آيات) that Allah sent to the House of Pharaoh. The apparent context is that He sent what He sent of them separately, part by part; hence He used the plural "years," and the plural is only realized with a separation between one year and another. Furthermore, He says: فَإِذا جاءَتْهُمُ الْحَسَنَةُ قالُوا لَنا هذِهِ **"When good times came to them, they would say, ‘This is our due’"** (7:131). The apparent meaning is the good that comes after the evil, then the evil that comes after this good.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَإِذا جاءَتْهُمُ الْحَسَنَةُ قالُوا لَنا هذِهِ **"When good times came to them, they would say, ‘This is our due’"** until the end of the verse. When fertility, abundance of blessings, and spaciousness of provision came to them after the lifting of the famine and the loss of fruits, they would say: لَنا هذِهِ **"This is our due,"** intending by it exclusivity. We only said that they used to say that after the lifting of the famine and loss of fruits because man, by nature (الطبع), does not turn to the mention of a blessing (النعمة) as a blessing, nor does he become aware of its value, except after witnessing the affliction (النقمة) which is its opposite. There would be no motive for the House of Pharaoh to mention the good blessing and claim it for themselves had they not seen its opposite and considered it a strange matter they had not seen before. Thus, they drew an evil omen (اطيروا) from Moses and those with him, then when the evil was replaced by good, they attributed it to themselves. So, the drawing of evil omens (التطير) at the time of evil, in terms of occurrence, precedes their saying regarding the good: "This is our due," even if the matter according to nature is the opposite—meaning that had they not presumed and had it not been ingrained in their souls from their habituation to luxury, abundance of blessing, and fertility that they were specifically entitled to it and possessed it, they would not have drawn an evil omen from Moses upon the descent of calamity upon them. For he who is not comforted by ease and well-being does not feel distressed by their opposites.

Perhaps this is the reason for the Exalted mentioning their delusion with the blessing before their drawing of evil omens at the time of affliction. Then He mentioned the "good" (الحسنة) with the word "when" (فَإِذا) and the "evil" (السيئة) with the word "if" (إِنْ), where He said: **"When good times came to them, they would say, ‘This is our due,’ but if an ill visited them, they would take it as an evil omen for Moses and those with him."** He made the coming of the good like a fixed principle, mentioning it with *idha* (إذا) and the definite article of genus (لام الجنس), then mentioned the affliction of evil by way of a condition, and left "evil" (سيئة) indefinite to indicate its rarity and its being incidental.

The word *tatayyur* (التطير) — *the drawing of evil omens* — is derived from *tayr* (الطير) — *birds* — considering it includes a certain relationship, which is the relationship of pessimism (التشؤم). They used to be pessimistic about certain birds like the crow, so from it was derived that which yields the meaning of pessimism, which is *tatayyur*. Its meaning is to be pessimistic by means of birds, until absolute lot, or the lot of evil and misfortune, was called a "bird" (طائراً).

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: أَلا إِنَّما طائِرُهُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ **"Look! Their ill omen is only with Allah, but most of them do not know"** means that their lot of evil and misfortune which deserves to be called the lot of evil—which is the punishment—is with Allah, but most of them do not know because of their assumption that what their hands earn passes away and vanishes and is not preserved against them.

Other meanings for "bird" (الطائر) in the verse are sometimes mentioned, such as the book of deeds which Allah called a "bird," and other things, but the most suitable to the context is what has preceded.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَقالُوا مَهْما تَأْتِنا بِهِ مِنْ آيَةٍ لِتَسْحَرَنا بِها فَما نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ **"They said, ‘Whatever sign you may bring us to bewitch us with, we will not believe in you.’"** The word *mahma* (مهما) is among the conditional nouns meaning "whatever thing." This saying of theirs was to make Moses despair of them ever believing in him, even if he brought any sign. In their saying: مِنْ آيَةٍ لِتَسْحَرَنا بِها **"of a sign to bewitch us with,"** there is mockery of him, as they called it a "sign" while making his purpose for it to bewitch them—meaning: "You bring us sorcery and call it a sign."

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَأَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمُ الطُّوفانَ وَالْجَرادَ وَالْقُمَّلَ وَالضَّفادِعَ وَالدَّمَ آياتٍ مُفَصَّلاتٍ **"So We sent against them the flood and the locusts, the lice, the frogs and blood, as distinct signs..."** until the end of the verse. The "flood" (الطوفان) — according to what al-Raghib said — is every incident that surrounds (تحيط) man, and it became conventionally used for water that is extreme in abundance. In *al-Majma'*: it is the torrent that encompasses the earth with its drowning, and it is taken from "circling" (الطوف) within it. [End of quote].

The "lice" (القمل) — with a *dammah* and *shaddah* — it is said: large ticks, and it is said: small flies; and with a *fathah* then *sukun*, it is well-known. The locusts, frogs, and blood are well-known.

"Detailing" (التفصيل) is the dividing of a thing into distinct parts separated from one another. A necessary consequence of this is the distinction of each part and its manifestation in itself. Thus, His saying: آياتٍ مُفَصَّلاتٍ **"distinct signs"** indicates that they were sent to them not collectively and all at once, but separately and distinctly from one another, clearly being divine signs intended, not incidental or haphazard.

Among the evidence for "distinct" (المفصلات) being in this sense is His saying in the following verse: وَلَمَّا وَقَعَ عَلَيْهِمُ الرِّجْزُ قالُوا **"When the punishment fell upon them, they said..."** until the end of the verse. It appears that the sign would come to them following information from Moses and a warning, then when it descended upon them and overwhelmed them, they would resort to him and ask him to pray for them so it would be lifted from them, and they gave him a covenant that if it were lifted from them, they would believe in him and send the Children of Israel with him. Then when it was lifted, they would breach and break [the covenant], and so on.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَلَمَّا وَقَعَ عَلَيْهِمُ الرِّجْزُ قالُوا يا مُوسَى ادْعُ لَنا رَبَّكَ **"When the punishment fell upon them, they said, ‘O Moses! Invoke your Lord for us...’"** until the end of the verse. The "punishment" (الرجز) is the torment, and it refers to the torment that each one of the distinct signs contained, for they were signs of torment and exemplary punishment. His saying: بِما عَهدَ عِنْدَكَ **"by the covenant He has made with you"** — as supported by the context — means: by what He has committed to you that He will not reject your prayer in what you ask of Him. The *lam* in that case is for an oath, and the meaning is: "Invoke your Lord for us by the covenant He has with you."

His saying: لَئِنْ كَشَفْتَ عَنَّا الرِّجْزَ لَنُؤْمِنَنَّ لَكَ وَلَنُرْسِلَنَّ مَعَكَ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ **"‘If you remove the punishment from us, we will surely believe in you and we will surely send the Children of Israel with you’"** is what they covenanted with Moses for the removal of the punishment from them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنا عَنْهُمُ الرِّجْزَ إِلى أَجَلٍ هُمْ بالِغُوهُ إِذا هُمْ يَنْكُثُونَ **"But when We removed the punishment from them until a term they were to complete, behold, they would break their word."** "Breaching" (النكث) is the breaking of a covenant. His saying: إِلى أَجَلٍ هُمْ بالِغُوهُ **"until a term they were to complete"** is connected to His saying: كَشَفْنا **"We removed."** It indicates that there was joined to the covenant a set term, as if Moses (peace be upon him) would say: "Allah will lift the torment from you on condition that you believe and send the Children of Israel with me until such a term," or the House of Pharaoh would say something similar to this meaning. Then when the torment was lifted from them and the set term arrived, they breached and broke their covenant which they had made with Allah and with Moses. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْناهُمْ فِي الْيَمِ **"So We took vengeance on them and drowned them in the sea."** The "sea" (اليم) is the ocean, and the rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَأَوْرَثْنَا الْقَوْمَ الَّذِينَ كانُوا يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشارِقَ الْأَرْضِ وَمَغارِبَهَا **"We made the people who were abased inherit the easts and wests of the land..."** until the end of the verse. It appears that what is meant by "the land" is the land of the Levant (الشام) and Palestine. This is supported or indicated by His saying afterward: الَّتِي بارَكْنا فِيها **"which We had blessed,"** for Allah, the Glorified, has not mentioned with blessing any land other than the Holy Land which is the regions of Palestine, except for what He described the blessed Kaaba with. The meaning is: We gave the Children of Israel—and they are the ones who were abased—inheritance of the Holy Land in its easts and its wests. He only mentioned them by their description, saying: "the people who were abased," to indicate His wondrous handiwork, the Exalted, in raising the lowly, strengthening the abased, and giving him possession of the land the likes of which is normally only possible for the powerful who possess supporters and helpers.

His saying: وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ الْحُسْنى **"and the best word of your Lord was fulfilled..."** until the end of the verse. He means by it what He had decreed in their regard—that He would give them inheritance of the land and destroy their enemy. To this Moses (peace be upon him) alluded in his saying to them while consoling them and confirming their hope: عَسى رَبُّكُمْ أَنْ يُهْلِكَ عَدُوَّكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ **"Maybe your Lord will destroy your enemy and make you successors in the land."** And the Glorified alludes to it in His saying: **"And We desired to show favor to those who were abased in the land, and to make them imams, and to make them the heirs"** (28:5). The "fulfillment of the word" (تمام الكلمة) is its transition from the stage of potentiality to the stage of actuality. He justified that by their patience.

His saying: وَدَمَّرْنا ما كانَ يَصْنَعُ فِرْعَوْنُ وَقَوْمُهُ **"and We destroyed what Pharaoh and his people had been crafting..."** until the end of the verse. That is, We destroyed what they used to craft and what they used to roof of palaces and buildings, and what they used to trellis of vineyards and other things.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*: Ibn Abbas, Sa'id ibn Jubayr, Qatadah, and Muhammad ibn Ishaq ibn Bashar said—and Ali ibn Ibrahim narrated it with his chain from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them), the narration of some being interwoven with others—they said: "When the sorcerers believed and Pharaoh returned defeated—and he and his people refused anything but remaining in disbelief—Haman said to Pharaoh: 'The people have believed in Moses, so look for whoever has entered his religion and imprison him.' So he imprisoned everyone who believed in him from the Children of Israel. Then Allah followed up against them with the signs, and afflicted them with droughts and loss of fruits.

Then He sent against them the flood, which destroyed their houses and dwellings until they went out to the wilderness and pitched tents. The houses of the Copts were filled with water, while not a drop of water entered the houses of the Children of Israel. It remained upon the surface of their lands such that they were unable to plow. So they said to Moses: 'Invoke your Lord for us to lift the rain from us, and we will believe in you and send the Children of Israel with you.' So he invoked his Lord and He lifted the flood from them, but they did not believe. Haman said to Pharaoh: 'If you let the Children of Israel go, Moses will overcome you and remove your kingdom.' And Allah caused to grow for them in that year such herbage, crops, and fruits that their lands became lush and fertile. So they said: 'This water was nothing but a blessing upon us and fertility.'

Then Allah sent down upon them in the second year—according to Ali ibn Ibrahim—and in the second month—according to other commentators—the locusts. They stripped their crops and trees until they would strip their hair and beards, and eat the garments, clothes, and belongings. They would not enter the houses of the Children of Israel, nor would anything of that afflict them. So they cried out, clamored, and Pharaoh was severely distressed by that. He said: 'O Moses! Invoke your Lord for us to lift the locusts from us, until I release the Children of Israel.' So Moses invoked his Lord and He lifted the locusts from them, after they had remained upon them for seven days, from Saturday to Saturday.

It is said: Moses went out to the open space and pointed with his staff toward the east and the west, and the locusts returned from whence they came until it was as if they had never been. But Haman did not let Pharaoh release the Children of Israel.

Then Allah sent down upon them in the third year—in the narration of Ali ibn Ibrahim—and in the third month—according to other commentators—the lice (القمل), which are small locusts that have no wings, and they are the worst and most wicked of things. They came upon all their crops and uprooted them from their source, so their crops vanished, and they licked the entire earth.

It is said: Moses was commanded to walk to a dust-colored mound in a village among the villages of Egypt called Ayn Shams. He came to it and struck it with his staff, and it poured down lice upon them. They would enter between a person's garment and bite him, and one would eat food and it would be filled with lice. Sa'id ibn Jubayr said: *al-Qaml* is the weevil that comes out of grains. A man would take ten sacks to the mill and not return with three measures from them. They were not afflicted with a trial more severe upon them than the lice. It took their hair, their eyes, their eyelashes, and their eyebrows, and clung to their skins as if it were smallpox upon them. it prevented them from sleep and rest, so they screamed and cried out. Pharaoh said to Moses: 'Invoke your Lord for us; if you remove the lice from us, I will surely cease [oppressing] the Children of Israel.' So Moses invoked until the lice went away, after they had remained with them for seven days, from Saturday to Saturday. Then they breached [the covenant].

Then Allah sent down upon them in the fourth year—and it is said: in the fourth month—the frogs. They would be in their food and drink, and their houses and buildings were filled with them. No one would uncover a garment, a vessel, food, or drink without finding frogs in it. They would jump into their cooking pots and spoil what was in them. A man would sit with frogs up to his chin, and he would intend to speak and a frog would jump into his mouth; he would open his mouth for his morsel and the frog would precede his morsel into his mouth. They met severe harm from them. When they saw that, they wept and complained of that to Moses, saying: 'This time we repent and will not return; so invoke Allah to take away the frogs from us, for we will believe in you and send the Children of Israel with you.' So he took their covenants and treaties, then invoked his Lord, and He removed the frogs from them, after they had remained upon them for seven [days], from Saturday to Saturday. Then they broke the covenant and returned to their disbelief.

When it was the fifth year, Allah sent against them the blood. The water of the Nile flowed as blood upon them. The Copt would see it as blood, while the Israelite would see it as water. If the Israelite drank it, it was water, and if the Copt drank it, it was blood. The Copt would say to the Israelite: 'Take the water in your mouth and pour it into my mouth.' But when he poured it into the mouth of the Copt, it would turn into blood. Pharaoh was seized by thirst until he was forced to chew moist trees, and when he chewed them, their juice in his mouth became blood. They remained in that state for seven days, eating nothing but blood and drinking nothing but blood. Zayd ibn Aslam said: The blood that was set upon them was nosebleeds (الرعاف). So they came to Moses and said: 'Invoke your Lord for us to remove this blood from us, and we will believe in you and send the Children of Israel with you.' When Allah repelled the blood from them, they did not believe, nor did they release the Children of Israel."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Muhammad ibn Qays from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding: لَئِنْ كَشَفْتَ عَنَّا الرِّجْزَ لَنُؤْمِنَنَّ لَكَ **"‘If you remove the punishment from us, we will surely believe in you,’"** he said: "**The punishment** (الرجز) is snow." Then he said: "The lands of Khorasan are lands of *rijz*."

**I say:** The narration does not correspond to the verse in that specific way.
